question: Question 1. Tell me about yourself
answer:
I'm a Backend Team Lead with 8 years of experience building backend systems. Most of my experience is with NodeJS, NestJS, Express, Koa, PostgreSQL, DynamoDB, Redis, AWS, and Go. During the last few years I've also worked with AI technologies like LangChain, OpenAI, Claude Code, and MCP.

As a Team Lead, my work is not only writing code. I design system architecture, review pull requests, make technical decisions, mentor developers, and work closely with product managers to deliver features on time.

In our projects we use a NestJS monorepo architecture with shared libraries. This helps us keep the code consistent and easier to maintain as the team grows.

One project I'm proud of was an AI business planning platform. I designed part of the backend architecture, integrated LangChain with OpenAI, generated PDF reports, and built scalable APIs. I also helped introduce AI into our development process using Claude Code for planning, code generation, and code reviews.

I enjoy solving complex backend problems, improving architecture, and helping engineers grow. My goal is always to build reliable systems that are easy to maintain and easy to scale.

question: Question 2. What is the difference between REST and GraphQL?
answer:
REST and GraphQL are two different ways to build APIs. Both solve the same problem, but they have different strengths.

In most of our projects we use REST. It has clear endpoints, uses standard HTTP methods like GET, POST, PUT, and DELETE, and is easy to cache, document, and debug. It also works very well with tools like Swagger and Postman.

GraphQL uses a single endpoint. Instead of receiving a fixed response, the client requests exactly the fields it needs. This reduces over-fetching and under-fetching, especially when different clients need different data.

We choose REST because it is simple, predictable, and easier for large teams to maintain. Most business applications don't need the flexibility of GraphQL.

If I were building a complex frontend with many dashboards or mobile applications that request different data, I would consider GraphQL. Otherwise, REST is usually the better choice because it is simpler and easier to support over time.

question: Question 3. Describe a challenging technical problem you solved
answer:
One of the most interesting performance problems I solved happened in an events service that used DynamoDB.

At the beginning everything worked well because the database contained only a small amount of data. After a few weeks the API became slower and eventually some requests timed out. Response times increased from under one second to almost ten seconds.

I started by checking CloudWatch logs and measuring the execution time of every step. I found that every request was using DynamoDB Scan without pagination. Scan reads the whole table, so as the data grew, every request became more expensive.

We redesigned the queries around our access patterns, replaced Scan with Query, created the required indexes, and added cursor-based pagination. After deployment the average response time dropped from 8 to 10 seconds to under 200 milliseconds.

The biggest lesson I learned is that performance problems often come from the data access layer. Choosing the correct database operation and designing indexes early can save a lot of time later.

question: Question 4. How do you handle authentication?
answer:
The authentication solution depends on the project. I've worked with JWT, Auth0, Amazon Cognito, and Basic Authentication, but most of our projects use JWT.

When a user logs in successfully, the backend creates an access token with an expiration time. Every request includes that token, and middleware verifies it before the request reaches the business logic.

Authentication answers the question 'Who is this user?'. Authorization answers 'What is this user allowed to do?'. After authentication we apply role-based access control so users can only access the resources they are permitted to use.

We never store secrets inside the code. In production we keep them in AWS Secrets Manager, and in CI/CD we use GitHub Secrets.

As a Team Lead I also focus on security best practices. We use short-lived tokens, HTTPS everywhere, proper validation, and regular permission reviews because security is part of the architecture, not something added later.
bonus: Authentication checks who the user is. Authorization checks what the user can do.

question: Question 5. How do you handle a large number of concurrent requests?
answer:
Handling many concurrent requests starts with good system design, not just adding more servers.

In our projects we keep services stateless. Because no user session is stored inside the application, we can easily run multiple instances behind a load balancer.

We also optimize the database layer by creating proper indexes and caching frequently requested data in Redis. This reduces the number of expensive database queries.

Long-running operations such as file processing, report generation, or video processing are moved to background workers or AWS Lambda instead of making users wait for the HTTP request to finish.

In one project users uploaded large files to Amazon S3. Instead of processing them immediately, S3 triggered a Lambda function that handled the work asynchronously. The API returned almost instantly while processing continued in the background.

The biggest lesson I've learned is that scalability comes from many small decisions working together: good architecture, efficient queries, caching, asynchronous processing, and continuous monitoring.

question: Question 6. How do you structure a backend project?
answer:
Good project structure is important because it makes the code easier to understand, test, and maintain. As a Team Lead, I always try to keep the architecture simple and consistent.

In our projects we mainly use NestJS with a monorepo architecture. Each module has its own controller, service, repository, DTOs, and entities. Controllers are responsible only for handling HTTP requests. Business logic stays inside services, and repositories work with the database.

We also keep shared code like utilities, authentication, logging, configuration, and common models in shared libraries. This avoids duplication and keeps the codebase consistent across services.

For larger systems we split the application into multiple services, but we don't start with microservices unless there is a real business need. A well-structured monolith is usually easier to develop and maintain.

We validate every request, document APIs with Swagger, store secrets outside the application, and follow the same coding standards across the whole team. Good architecture helps new developers become productive much faster.

question: Question 7. How does the NodeJS Event Loop work?
answer:
NodeJS runs JavaScript in a single main thread, but it can still handle thousands of requests because of the Event Loop.

When a request needs an I/O operation, like reading from PostgreSQL, DynamoDB, Redis, or calling another API, NodeJS doesn't wait. Instead, it gives the work to the operating system or libuv and immediately starts processing another request.

When the operation finishes, the callback is placed into the event queue. The Event Loop checks whether the call stack is empty, and if it is, it executes the callback.

This is why NodeJS performs very well for APIs and other I/O-heavy applications. However, CPU-intensive work can block the Event Loop and delay every other request.

In our projects we move heavy work like video processing, large file imports, or AI document generation to AWS Lambda, worker threads, or background services. The biggest lesson I've learned is to keep the Event Loop free so it can continue serving new requests quickly.
bonus: Event Loop phases — timers → I/O callbacks → setImmediate → close callbacks.

question: Question 8. How do you handle asynchronous operations?
answer:
Today we write almost all asynchronous code with async and await because it is clean, easy to read, and easier to maintain than callbacks or promise chains.

Every database query, API request, file upload, or message queue operation is asynchronous. We wrap these operations in try/catch blocks so errors are handled correctly and users receive meaningful responses.

When independent operations can run at the same time, we use Promise.all to reduce response time. For example, if we need to load user information, organization settings, and feature flags, we don't wait for each request to finish before starting the next one.

If some operations are optional, we use Promise.allSettled because one failure should not always stop the whole request.

As a Team Lead, I also pay attention to error handling and logging. Fast code is important, but predictable behavior is even more important. Good asynchronous code should be easy to understand, easy to debug, and safe when something fails.

question: Question 9. What databases do you use and when?
answer:
I don't believe there is one perfect database for every project. The right choice depends on the business requirements, the data model, and the expected traffic.

In our projects we use PostgreSQL for structured business data such as users, payments, orders, and financial transactions. PostgreSQL supports ACID transactions, strong consistency, indexes, and complex SQL queries, which makes it a great choice for transactional systems.

For high-scale applications with predictable access patterns we use DynamoDB. It provides excellent performance and automatic scaling, but it requires careful table and index design. One project became much faster after we replaced DynamoDB Scan with Query and designed proper indexes.

Redis is not our primary database. We use it as a cache layer for frequently requested data, user sessions, rate limiting, and temporary values. Reading from memory is much faster than querying the database every time.

As a Team Lead, I choose databases based on consistency, scalability, performance, and cost instead of using the same technology everywhere.
bonus: Why not store everything in Redis? Memory costs more. The real data always stays in PostgreSQL or DynamoDB.

question: Question 10. How do you use Redis for caching?
answer:
In our projects Redis is mainly used as a cache to reduce database load and improve response times.

The most common pattern we use is cache-aside. First we check Redis. If the data exists, we return it immediately. If it doesn't, we read it from PostgreSQL or DynamoDB, store it in Redis with a TTL, and return the result to the client.

When data changes, we either update the cached value or remove it so the next request loads fresh data from the database. Cache invalidation is very important because outdated data can create difficult bugs.

Besides caching, we also use Redis for user sessions, rate limiting, distributed locks, and temporary data shared between services.

One lesson I've learned is that caching should solve a real performance problem. I always measure database performance first. If the queries are already fast enough, adding Redis only increases system complexity without providing much value.
bonus: Other strategies: Write-Through (write to cache and database at the same time), Write-Behind (write to cache first, sync to database later).

question: Question 11. How do you design a REST API?
answer:
When I design a REST API, my main goals are simplicity, consistency, and maintainability. A good API should be easy for developers to understand without reading a lot of documentation.

In our projects we use standard HTTP methods. GET reads data, POST creates resources, PUT or PATCH updates them, and DELETE removes them. Resource names are nouns like /users or /orders instead of actions.

We also use proper HTTP status codes, request validation, pagination, filtering, sorting, and versioning. Every API is documented with Swagger so frontend and mobile developers can start working immediately.

As a Team Lead, I try to keep APIs consistent across all services. If every team follows different naming conventions or response formats, the system becomes difficult to maintain.

One lesson I've learned is that APIs live much longer than we expect. Spending extra time on good design at the beginning saves many hours of maintenance and prevents breaking changes in the future.
bonus: URLs should describe resources, not actions — GET /users is right, /getUsers is not.

question: Question 12. How do you version APIs?
answer:
API versioning allows us to improve an API without breaking existing clients. My goal is always to keep backward compatibility whenever possible.

In our projects we usually use URL versioning such as /v1/users and /v2/users. It is simple, easy to understand, and works well with documentation and API gateways.

We create a new version only when we introduce breaking changes, for example removing fields, changing response structures, or modifying business behavior. Adding optional fields normally does not require a new version.

Before removing an old version, we communicate with frontend and mobile teams and give them enough time to migrate.

One lesson I've learned is that supporting too many API versions increases maintenance costs. It's important to plan deprecation early and remove unused versions once every client has migrated.

question: Question 13. How do you implement pagination?
answer:
Pagination is important because APIs should never return unlimited amounts of data. Without pagination, response times become slower and databases work much harder.

The implementation depends on the database. For PostgreSQL we often use limit and offset pagination together with metadata such as total count, current page, and hasNextPage.

For DynamoDB we use cursor-based pagination with LastEvaluatedKey. Instead of page numbers, the client receives a cursor and sends it back to request the next page. This approach performs much better on very large datasets.

One project became much faster after replacing a DynamoDB Scan with Query and cursor-based pagination. Response time dropped from around 10 seconds to under 200 milliseconds.

As a Team Lead, I choose the pagination strategy based on performance and scalability, not just what is easiest to implement.
bonus: Offset pagination is simple. Cursor pagination is faster for large datasets.

question: Question 14. How do you implement filtering and sorting?
answer:
Filtering and sorting help clients request only the data they really need. A well-designed API should support these features without making the database slow.

In our projects we usually receive filters through query parameters like status=active, search=john, or sortBy=createdAt. The backend validates these values before building the query.

For PostgreSQL we create indexes on fields that are frequently filtered or sorted. Without proper indexes, even simple queries can become slow as the data grows.

For DynamoDB we design GSIs and LSIs based on our access patterns. Since DynamoDB is not designed for ad-hoc queries, planning indexes before development is very important.

One lesson I've learned is that filtering should be designed together with the database schema. A good API starts with a good data model.

question: Question 15. What is idempotency?
answer:
Idempotency means that sending the same request multiple times produces the same final result. This is especially important for payment systems and other critical operations.

Methods like GET, PUT, and DELETE are naturally idempotent. POST usually is not because sending the same request twice may create duplicate records.

In our payment projects we solve this by using an idempotency key. The client generates a unique key and sends it with the request. If the same request is received again, the backend returns the previous result instead of processing the payment again.

This approach protects users from duplicate charges caused by network failures or retries.

One lesson I've learned is that idempotency should be considered during API design, not added later after duplicate transactions have already happened.
bonus: GET, PUT, DELETE = idempotent. POST = usually not. PATCH = depends.

question: Question 16. How do you handle file uploads?
answer:
The upload strategy depends on the file size and the application architecture.

For small files we allow uploads through the backend API. The server validates the file type, size, and content before storing it in Amazon S3.

For large files we generate pre-signed URLs. The client uploads directly to S3 without sending the file through our backend. This reduces server load, improves upload speed, and works well with AWS Lambda where request sizes are limited.

In several projects we also configured S3 events to trigger Lambda functions after the upload finished. Those functions generated thumbnails, processed documents, or started AI workflows.

One lesson I've learned is that backend servers should not become file transfer services. Let S3 handle storage while the backend focuses on business logic.

question: Question 17. How do you design a payment API?
answer:
Payment APIs require much more than simply calling Stripe. The most important goals are security, consistency, and reliability.

In our projects we never trust the frontend to decide whether a payment succeeded. The backend creates the payment, stores its status in PostgreSQL, and waits for the final confirmation from Stripe webhooks.

We use database transactions when updating payment records, idempotency keys to prevent duplicate charges, and AWS Secrets Manager to store API credentials securely.

I've worked mostly with Stripe, including subscriptions, one-time payments, and webhook processing. We also separate payment logic into dedicated services so it remains easy to maintain and test.

As a Team Lead, I always treat payment systems as critical infrastructure. Small mistakes can cost real money, so reliability and clear audit history are more important than writing code quickly.

question: Question 18. How do you validate incoming requests?
answer:
Every request should be validated before it reaches the business logic. Good validation improves security, prevents unexpected errors, and keeps the application stable.

In our NestJS projects we define DTOs with class-validator decorators. We validate request bodies, query parameters, path parameters, and sometimes headers depending on the endpoint.

Frontend validation improves the user experience, but it can never replace backend validation because anyone can send requests directly using Postman or another HTTP client.

When validation fails, we return clear error messages with appropriate HTTP status codes so API consumers know exactly what needs to be fixed.

One lesson I've learned is that validation should be consistent across every service. Following the same validation rules everywhere makes APIs easier to use, easier to debug, and much more secure.

question: Question 19. How do database indexes work?
answer:
A database index works like the index at the back of a book. Instead of reading every page, the database can jump directly to the data it needs. This makes queries much faster.

In our projects we create indexes on columns that are frequently used for filtering, sorting, joining, or searching. For example, userId, organizationId, email, and createdAt are common candidates.

However, indexes are not free. Every insert, update, or delete must also update the indexes, so having too many indexes can slow down write operations and increase storage usage.

When a query becomes slow, I first check the execution plan using EXPLAIN. This helps me understand whether the database is using an index or performing a full table scan.

In one project we found that a slow API was caused by missing indexes combined with a DynamoDB Scan operation. After adding the correct indexes and replacing Scan with Query, response time dropped from around 8 to 10 seconds to under 200 milliseconds.

One lesson I've learned is to create indexes based on real query patterns, not assumptions.
bonus: Indexes make reads faster but writes a bit slower. Only index fields you actually filter or sort by.

question: Question 20. What is a database transaction?
answer:
A transaction groups multiple database operations into a single unit of work. Either every operation succeeds, or everything is rolled back. This keeps the data consistent even if something fails.

In our projects we use PostgreSQL transactions for critical business operations such as payments, donations, and financial records. If one step fails, we don't want half of the data to be saved.

For example, when processing a payment we may need to create a transaction record, calculate earnings, update balances, and save audit information. If any step fails, the whole transaction is rolled back.

Transactions follow the ACID principles: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, and Durability. These guarantees are very important when working with money or other critical business data.

As a Team Lead, I always ask whether an operation really needs a transaction. They improve consistency but also hold database locks longer, so they should only include the minimum amount of work.
bonus: If two transactions try to update the same row at the same time, isolation kicks in — one waits for the other to finish.

question: Question 21. How do you optimize slow SQL queries?
answer:
When I investigate a slow SQL query, I try to find the root cause instead of guessing. Optimization should always be based on measurements.

The first step is checking the execution plan with EXPLAIN or EXPLAIN ANALYZE. This shows whether indexes are being used and which part of the query takes the most time.

Next I look for missing indexes, unnecessary joins, full table scans, or queries returning too much data. We also avoid SELECT * and only request the columns we actually need.

Pagination is another important optimization. Returning thousands of rows when the user only needs twenty wastes memory, CPU, and network bandwidth.

If the same data is requested very often, we cache it in Redis instead of querying the database every time.

One lesson I've learned is that most performance problems are solved by better queries and better indexes, not by buying a bigger database server.

question: Question 22. How do you investigate a slow API?
answer:
When an API becomes slow, I follow a structured process instead of making assumptions.

First I check whether the problem affects one endpoint or the whole application. Then I look at CloudWatch metrics, logs, CPU usage, memory, database connections, and recent deployments.

If only one endpoint is affected, I measure every step separately. I check database queries, Redis cache, external API calls, business logic, and serialization time. The goal is to find the exact bottleneck.

One real example was an events API that became slower as the database grew. After investigating the logs and measuring execution times, I found that every request used DynamoDB Scan without pagination. We redesigned the access pattern, switched to Query, added indexes, and implemented cursor pagination. Response time improved from about 8 to 10 seconds to under 200 milliseconds.

The biggest lesson I've learned is to measure first and optimize second. Data should drive every performance decision.
bonus: Real example: DynamoDB Scan without pagination. Changed to Query — response time went from 8 to 10 seconds to under 200 milliseconds.

question: Question 23. What is the N+1 query problem?
answer:
The N+1 query problem happens when an application loads the main data with one query and then performs an additional query for every returned record. This creates many unnecessary database calls.

For example, loading 100 orders and then querying the customer for each order results in 101 database queries instead of one or two.

In our projects we solve this in different ways depending on the situation. For SQL databases we often use JOINs or batch loading with WHERE IN. For GraphQL applications we can use DataLoader to batch requests automatically.

I also monitor query counts during development because the N+1 problem is easy to miss when the database contains only a small amount of data.

One lesson I've learned is that applications usually work well during development, but the N+1 problem becomes very expensive when production data grows. Finding it early saves a lot of future performance problems.

question: Question 24. What caching strategies do you know?
answer:
Caching improves performance by storing frequently requested data closer to the application. However, I only introduce caching after measuring that it solves a real problem.

The strategy we use most often is Cache-Aside. The application checks Redis first. If the data is missing, it loads it from PostgreSQL or DynamoDB, stores it in Redis with a TTL, and returns it to the client.

We also use CDN caching with CloudFront for static files like images, videos, and documents. This reduces latency for users around the world.

Whenever data changes, we update or invalidate the cache to avoid serving outdated information. Cache invalidation is usually harder than caching itself.

One lesson I've learned is that caching should improve performance without reducing data consistency. A fast system that returns incorrect information is still a bad system.

question: Question 25. How do you handle millions of records?
answer:
Processing millions of records inside a single HTTP request is not a good design. The request would probably time out and block server resources.

In our projects we treat large imports as background jobs. The client uploads the file to Amazon S3, the API creates a processing job with a Pending status, and immediately returns HTTP 202.

An AWS Lambda function or background worker processes the file in small batches. This approach keeps memory usage low and makes it possible to retry only the failed batches if necessary.

During processing we save progress, log errors, and update the job status so users can monitor the import. When everything is finished, they receive the final report with successful and failed records.

One lesson I've learned is that scalability is usually achieved by breaking large tasks into many small, independent operations instead of trying to process everything at once.

question: Question 27. Which AWS services have you used?
answer:
I've worked with AWS for several years on different backend projects. We use AWS because it provides reliable managed services that allow us to focus on building business features instead of managing infrastructure.

For compute I've used Lambda, EC2, and API Gateway. For storage we use S3. For databases we use PostgreSQL on RDS, DynamoDB, and ElastiCache with Redis. For monitoring we use CloudWatch, and for security we use IAM and Secrets Manager. I've also worked with EventBridge, SNS, Athena, GuardDuty, Amazon IVS, Bedrock, and ECR.

One project used Lambda together with S3 events to process uploaded files automatically. Another project used IVS for live video streaming and Bedrock for AI integrations.

As a Team Lead I don't choose AWS services because they are popular. I choose them based on scalability, operational cost, maintenance effort, and business requirements. The best architecture is usually the simplest one that solves the problem reliably.

question: Question 28. Why do you use AWS Lambda?
answer:
AWS Lambda is a good choice for event-driven workloads where we don't want to manage servers. AWS automatically handles scaling, availability, and infrastructure.

In our projects we use Lambda for background jobs, scheduled tasks, API endpoints, file processing, and AI workflows. For example, after a user uploads a file to S3, a Lambda function starts automatically, processes the file, stores the result, and updates the job status.

Lambda also works well for applications with unpredictable traffic because we only pay when the function runs.

However, Lambda is not the right solution for everything. Cold starts, the 15-minute execution limit, and memory constraints make it less suitable for long-running services or heavy background processing.

One lesson I've learned is that serverless is a great tool, but not every application should be serverless. The architecture should always match the business requirements.
bonus: Lambda is great for event-driven work. For long-running tasks EC2 or ECS is usually a better fit.

question: Question 29. How do you use Amazon S3?
answer:
Amazon S3 is our main object storage service. We use it for uploaded files, documents, images, videos, backups, and generated reports.

For security, buckets are private by default. Instead of exposing files directly, we generate pre-signed URLs that allow temporary access. This reduces the load on our backend because clients upload files directly to S3.

We also integrate S3 with CloudFront to deliver static content faster around the world. For background processing we use S3 events that automatically trigger AWS Lambda functions.

One project generated PDF business plans with AI. After the document was created, it was stored in S3 and users downloaded it through secure pre-signed URLs.

One lesson I've learned is that the backend should not become a file server. S3 is built for storing files reliably and at scale, so we let it do that job.

question: Question 30. How do you manage secrets and sensitive configuration?
answer:
Keeping secrets secure is an important part of backend development. API keys, database passwords, and private tokens should never be stored in source code or committed to Git.

In production we use AWS Secrets Manager. Applications retrieve secrets during startup or when they are needed. In CI/CD pipelines we use GitHub Secrets so credentials are injected only during deployment.

Each environment has its own configuration. Development, staging, and production all use different credentials and different resources.

As a Team Lead I also encourage regular secret rotation and the principle of least privilege. Services should only receive the permissions they actually need.

One lesson I've learned is that most security incidents are caused by simple mistakes. Following basic security practices consistently is much more valuable than using complicated security solutions.

question: Question 31. Why do you use Docker?
answer:
Docker allows us to package an application together with its dependencies so it runs the same way in every environment. This removes the classic 'it works on my machine' problem.

In our projects every service has its own Dockerfile. Developers use Docker Compose locally to run PostgreSQL, Redis, and backend services with one command. In production we build Docker images, push them to Amazon ECR, and deploy them through our CI/CD pipeline.

We also use multi-stage Docker builds to reduce image size and improve security. Smaller images are faster to build, upload, and deploy.

As a Team Lead I care about reproducible deployments. If every environment runs exactly the same Docker image, production becomes much more predictable.

One lesson I've learned is that Docker is not just a deployment tool. It also improves development, testing, and onboarding for the whole team.
bonus: Docker vs VM? A VM includes a full OS — heavy and slow. Docker shares the host OS — lighter and starts in seconds.

question: Question 32. How does CI/CD work?
answer:
CI/CD automates the process of building, testing, and deploying software. The goal is to deliver changes safely and frequently with minimal manual work.

In our projects we mainly use GitHub Actions. Every pull request runs linting, unit tests, and other validation steps. After merging, the pipeline builds Docker images or Lambda packages, publishes them, and deploys them automatically.

Development and staging deployments happen automatically. Production deployments require manual approval because they affect real users.

We also use Terraform to manage infrastructure as code, so infrastructure changes follow the same review process as application code.

One lesson I've learned is that a deployment process should be predictable. If deployments require many manual steps, mistakes become much more likely.

question: Question 33. How do you deploy backend services?
answer:
Our deployment process is fully automated. Every change goes through the CI/CD pipeline before reaching production.

For containerized applications we build a Docker image, push it to Amazon ECR, and deploy the new version. For serverless applications we package and deploy Lambda functions. Database migrations run automatically as part of the deployment process.

Before production deployments we run automated tests and perform code reviews. Production releases require manual approval, and after deployment we monitor logs and metrics to make sure everything is healthy.

If something goes wrong, we can quickly roll back to the previous version because every deployment is versioned.

One lesson I've learned is that reliable deployments are built on automation. The fewer manual steps there are, the fewer production problems the team will have.

question: Question 34. How do you monitor backend applications?
answer:
Monitoring helps us detect problems before users report them. Good monitoring is just as important as writing good code.

In our projects we use CloudWatch for logs, metrics, dashboards, and alarms. We monitor response time, error rate, CPU usage, memory, database connections, and request count. For business-critical services we also monitor business metrics such as payment failures or completed imports.

Alerts are sent to Slack or Telegram so the team is notified immediately when something unusual happens. We also expose health check endpoints that load balancers and monitoring systems can verify automatically.

When investigating incidents, logs and metrics are always the first place I look.

One lesson I've learned is that collecting data is not enough. Monitoring is valuable only when alerts are meaningful and help the team react quickly to real problems.

question: Question 26. Monolith vs Microservices
answer:
I've worked with both monoliths and microservices, and I don't think one architecture is always better than the other. The right choice depends on the team, the product, and the business goals.

For small and medium-sized projects, I usually prefer a well-structured monolith. It is easier to develop, deploy, test, and debug. New developers can also understand the system much faster.

As the system grows, different parts may need to scale independently. In that case, microservices become a good option. Each service owns its business logic and can be deployed separately. Services usually communicate through HTTP, gRPC, or asynchronous messaging using RabbitMQ or Kafka.

In our projects we started with a NestJS monorepo instead of microservices. This allowed us to move quickly while keeping the architecture clean. Later, if necessary, individual modules could be extracted into separate services.

One lesson I've learned is that many teams move to microservices too early. Good architecture is about solving business problems, not following trends.
bonus: I wouldn't use microservices for a small project or a small team. They add a lot of extra work.

question: Question 35. How do services communicate with each other?
answer:
The communication method depends on how quickly another service needs the result.

For synchronous communication we usually use HTTP REST or sometimes gRPC. The calling service waits for a response before continuing. This works well when the client needs the result immediately.

For asynchronous communication we use message brokers such as RabbitMQ or Kafka. One service publishes an event and another service processes it later. This approach is useful for sending emails, processing files, generating reports, or other background tasks.

In our projects we also use AWS services like SNS and EventBridge for event-driven workflows.

As a Team Lead I always ask whether synchronous communication is really necessary. Too many direct dependencies make systems harder to scale and recover from failures.

One lesson I've learned is to keep services loosely coupled whenever possible. Independent services are easier to deploy, scale, and maintain.
bonus: Sync when we need the result right away. Async for background work like emails or notifications.

question: Question 36. How do you do code review?
answer:
I believe code review is one of the most valuable engineering practices because it improves both code quality and knowledge sharing.

In our team every feature is developed in its own branch and submitted as a pull request. I encourage developers to keep pull requests small because they are much easier to review.

During review I don't only look for bugs. I also check readability, architecture, naming, performance, security, error handling, and whether the solution follows our coding standards.

We also use AI tools like Claude Code to perform an initial review before another engineer reads the code. This helps catch simple issues early, but the final decision is always made by a developer.

One lesson I've learned is that good code reviews should teach, not criticize. The goal is to improve both the code and the developer.

question: Question 37. How do you mentor junior developers?
answer:
I try to create an environment where junior developers feel comfortable asking questions and making mistakes. Learning happens much faster when people are not afraid to speak.

Before implementation we discuss the task together. Instead of immediately giving the answer, I ask the developer to explain their own solution first. This helps build problem-solving skills instead of simple copy-and-paste habits.

During code reviews I explain why I suggest a change instead of simply saying something is wrong. I also encourage pair programming for more complex tasks.

In our team we have shared coding standards, project documentation, and architecture guidelines so everyone follows the same approach.

One lesson I've learned is that mentoring is an investment. Spending extra time helping junior developers today saves much more time in the future.

question: Question 38. How do you make architecture decisions?
answer:
Architecture decisions should always start with understanding the business problem. Technology is only a tool to solve that problem.

When designing a new system I first collect the requirements, expected traffic, security needs, scalability requirements, and development timeline. Then I compare different approaches with the team.

In one project I designed a NestJS monorepo architecture with shared libraries because several applications needed common business logic. This reduced duplicated code and made maintenance much easier.

As a Team Lead I involve other engineers in important technical discussions. Different perspectives often lead to better solutions than decisions made by one person.

One lesson I've learned is that simple architecture usually wins. Every new technology adds operational cost, so I only introduce additional complexity when there is a clear business benefit.

question: Question 39. How do you estimate tasks?
answer:
I never estimate a task before I fully understand the requirements. The first step is asking questions and identifying unclear areas.

After that I break the work into smaller pieces such as backend development, database changes, testing, deployment, documentation, and code review. Smaller tasks are much easier to estimate accurately.

I also think about technical risks, dependencies, and possible unknowns. If a task involves new technology or external systems, I usually add some buffer because unexpected problems are common.

For larger features I discuss estimates with the team because another engineer may notice something I missed.

One lesson I've learned is that estimates are not promises. Good communication is more important than pretending every estimate will be exactly correct.

question: Question 40. How do you handle production incidents?
answer:
During a production incident my first priority is restoring the service as quickly as possible. Finding the root cause comes after the system is stable.

I start by checking monitoring dashboards, CloudWatch logs, recent deployments, and infrastructure metrics. If a recent release caused the problem, we immediately roll back to the previous version.

While the investigation continues, I keep the team informed so everyone understands the current status and knows who is working on each task.

After the incident is resolved, we hold a blameless post-mortem. We discuss what happened, why it happened, and what improvements will prevent similar incidents in the future.

One lesson I've learned is that good preparation is more important than reacting quickly. Strong monitoring, automated deployments, and clear runbooks make incidents much easier to manage.
bonus: After the incident we discuss what happened, why, and how to prevent it next time.

question: Question 41. Why should we hire you?
answer:
I bring both strong backend engineering experience and technical leadership.

Over the last eight years I've built production systems using NodeJS, NestJS, Go, PostgreSQL, DynamoDB, Redis, AWS, Stripe, and AI technologies such as LangChain and Claude Code. I've worked on payments, AI platforms, video streaming, file processing, and other backend systems.

As a Backend Team Lead I don't only write code. I design architecture, review pull requests, mentor developers, improve development processes, and help the team deliver reliable software.

I enjoy solving difficult technical problems, but I also understand that software development is a team activity. Good communication and collaboration are just as important as technical skills.

I believe I can contribute both by building scalable backend systems and by helping other engineers grow.

question: Question 42. What are your career goals?
answer:
In the short term I want to continue growing as a Backend Team Lead while improving my knowledge of distributed systems, cloud architecture, and AI integration.

I'm especially interested in building scalable platforms that help development teams work faster and deliver better software.

I've already worked with AI tools such as LangChain, OpenAI, Claude Code, and MCP, and I believe AI will become an important part of modern software development.

In the long term I would like to become a stronger technical leader who designs large systems, builds high-performing engineering teams, and helps companies solve complex technical challenges.

One lesson I've learned is that technology changes every year, but the ability to learn, communicate, and make good engineering decisions remains valuable throughout an entire career.

question: Question 43. How do you use AI in your daily work?
answer:
AI has become part of my daily development workflow. We mainly use Claude Code, but we also use OpenAI models when they fit the task better.

In our projects we use AI from the very beginning of a task. First, we ask it to analyze the requirements and create an implementation plan. We review that plan together before writing any code.

AI also helps with documentation, refactoring, unit tests, SQL queries, and explaining unfamiliar parts of the codebase. During code reviews it can identify simple mistakes before another developer reviews the pull request.

As a Team Lead I always remind the team that AI is a productivity tool, not a replacement for engineering judgment. Every generated change still goes through manual review, testing, and CI/CD.

One lesson I've learned is that the quality of AI output depends on the context you provide. Good project documentation and clear prompts produce much better results than asking AI to generate code without enough information.

question: Question 44. Which AI coding assistants have you used?
answer:
I've worked with several AI coding assistants, including Claude Code, OpenAI models, Cursor, and GitHub Copilot. Today Claude Code is our primary development tool because it fits our workflow very well.

In our projects we configure the AI before using it. We provide coding standards, project structure, architecture rules, and development guidelines so the generated code matches our existing codebase.

Different tools have different strengths. Cursor is convenient inside the IDE, GitHub Copilot is useful for code completion, and OpenAI models are great for brainstorming and documentation. Claude Code works especially well for larger implementation tasks because it understands project context very well.

As a Team Lead I encourage developers to use AI, but I also make sure everyone understands the generated code before merging it.

One lesson I've learned is that AI works best when it becomes part of the development process instead of replacing the developer.

question: Question 45. How do you use AI safely in production?
answer:
AI can generate code quickly, but production systems require strong engineering practices.

In our projects every AI-generated change follows exactly the same process as manually written code. It goes through code review, automated testing, security checks, and the CI/CD pipeline before deployment.

We never allow AI to deploy code directly to production or make decisions without developer approval. Sensitive areas like payments, authentication, security, and database migrations always receive additional manual review.

We also limit AI access to development resources. Production databases, secrets, and infrastructure are protected using IAM permissions and secret management.

As a Team Lead I treat AI like a junior developer who works very quickly. It can produce useful code, but every change must be reviewed carefully.

One lesson I've learned is that developer responsibility never changes. AI can assist with implementation, but engineers remain responsible for the final product.
bonus: The developer always makes the final decision — especially for security, payments, and database changes.

question: Question 46. How do you use Claude Code? Walk me through your workflow.
answer:
Our Claude Code workflow starts before writing any code.

First we maintain a CLAUDE.md file that explains our architecture, coding standards, project structure, naming conventions, and development rules. This gives Claude Code the context it needs.

When a new Jira task arrives, Claude Code analyzes the requirements and creates an implementation plan. We review the plan first and make changes if needed.

After approval, Claude Code generates the implementation step by step instead of creating one huge change. This makes code review much easier.

When development is finished, it helps prepare commits, create pull requests, and generate documentation. AI also performs an initial review before another engineer checks the code.

As a Team Lead I like this workflow because it keeps developers focused on architecture and business logic while AI handles many repetitive tasks.

One lesson I've learned is that planning before coding produces much better AI results than asking it to immediately generate code.

question: Question 47. What is MCP (Model Context Protocol)?
answer:
MCP stands for Model Context Protocol. It allows AI models to connect to external tools and systems using a standard interface.

Instead of copying information into the chat, AI can retrieve data directly from approved systems. This gives the model much better context while reducing manual work.

In our projects we use MCP servers for PostgreSQL, DynamoDB, and Jira. Claude Code can read database information, inspect schemas, and understand Jira tasks without developers manually copying everything.

Access is always controlled using existing security rules. AI only receives the permissions it actually needs, and production access remains restricted.

As a Team Lead I see MCP as an important improvement because it connects AI with the real development environment instead of treating it like a simple chatbot.

One lesson I've learned is that better context produces better answers. MCP significantly improves AI quality because it works with real project information.
bonus: We only give AI access to our development environment, never production.

question: Question 48. Have you built AI applications?
answer:
Yes. One of the most interesting projects I worked on was an AI-powered business planning platform.

The application starts with a conversation between the client and an AI assistant. The assistant asks questions, collects business information, and allows users to upload supporting documents.

On the backend we built the orchestration using LangChain together with large language models. The AI combines user answers with uploaded documents and generates a complete business plan.

After the content is generated, the system automatically creates a professionally formatted PDF that can be downloaded by the customer.

I was responsible for the backend architecture, AI integration, document generation, and workflow between different services.

One lesson I've learned is that successful AI applications require much more than calling an LLM API. Prompt design, validation, structured outputs, error handling, and user experience are equally important.

question: Question 49. How do you write good prompts?
answer:
For me, the most important part of prompt engineering is providing enough context.

Before asking AI to generate code, I describe the project, architecture, technologies, coding standards, and the exact business goal. If possible, I also include examples from our existing codebase.

I prefer breaking large tasks into smaller prompts instead of asking AI to generate a complete feature in one step. This produces more reliable results and makes code review much easier.

When the first answer isn't correct, I don't immediately start over. I explain what should change and continue improving the conversation.

As a Team Lead I encourage developers to think of prompts as technical specifications. Clear requirements produce better code whether the developer is a person or an AI.

One lesson I've learned is that better context almost always produces better AI responses.
bonus: The better the context and the prompt, the better the result.

question: Question 50. How do you review AI-generated code?
answer:
I review AI-generated code exactly the same way I review code written by another developer.

First I check whether the solution actually solves the business problem. After that I review architecture, readability, performance, security, error handling, and whether the implementation follows our coding standards.

I also verify edge cases, database queries, API contracts, and test coverage. AI can generate working code that still contains hidden performance or security problems.

In our projects AI performs an initial review of pull requests, but another developer always performs the final manual review before merging.

As a Team Lead I remind developers that understanding the code is more important than generating it quickly.

One lesson I've learned is that AI should speed up development, not reduce engineering quality. The final responsibility always belongs to the engineering team.
bonus: Never merge AI-generated code without reading and understanding it yourself.

question: Question 51. What are sub-agents and agent teams in Claude Code?
answer:
Sub-agents and agent teams are ways to break large tasks into parallel work streams.

A sub-agent is a separate Claude Code instance that handles one specific part of a task. For example, when implementing a new feature, the main agent can launch sub-agents in parallel — one writes the business logic, another writes the tests, and a third updates the documentation. When all sub-agents finish, the main agent combines the results.

This makes complex tasks significantly faster because work happens in parallel instead of one step at a time.

Agent teams take this further. Instead of one main agent with helpers, different specialized agents work together on a shared goal. One agent analyzes requirements, another designs the architecture, another writes the code, and another reviews it. They pass work to each other like a real development team.

In our workflow we already apply this pattern manually. We use separate Claude Code sessions for planning, implementation, and review. Each session gets the output from the previous one as context.

As a Team Lead I find this approach very useful for large features. Breaking the work into smaller, focused tasks produces better results than asking AI to do everything in one big request.

One lesson I've learned is that AI works best with clear, focused instructions. Sub-agents and agent teams help by giving each part of the work its own context and responsibility.
bonus: Anthropic is actively developing agent coordination features. In the future, agents will be able to distribute work, verify each other's output, and make decisions autonomously — similar to how a real engineering team operates.
